spec_match.rationale: Calls `navigator.cpuPerformance?.tier` with a heuristic fallback and binds particle/shadow/postFX/entity-budget to the returned tier, which is the canonical 'pick a preset once at startup' usage the WICG explainer endorses.

interactive.rationale: A live canvas mini-game with stars, bouncing entities, particle bursts, shadow blur, and a HUD showing FPS / entity count / frame budget — preset buttons swap the config in real time.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises a canvas game in low/mid/high presets with particles, shadows, post-FX, entity budget all binding to the tier plus an FPS counter; the page delivers all of those.

edge_cases.rationale: Auto-detect button is present but doesn't actually run the navigator.cpu path with proper feature detection visible to the user. No demo of: dropped frames behaviour, what happens when the page becomes hidden (RAF pauses), reduced-motion preference, or auto-downgrade when measured FPS lags target.

references.rationale: See-also section includes https://chromestatus.com/feature/5189864286978048 and the WICG explainer.

design_system.rationale: Canvas paints raw colours (`#060812`, `#cad2ff`, hsl/hsla without variables) — acceptable for canvas content as it's not part of the page chrome, but the inline `ctx.fillStyle` choices could be derived from CSS variables for theme consistency. Outside the canvas, all colours are variable-driven and the preset-btn override pairs background and color correctly.

openQuestions.title: Wire the auto-detect button to the real API
openQuestions.detail: The auto-detect preset button is in the UI but the page reads `navigator.cpuPerformance?.tier` once in static JS — clicking 'auto-detect' should re-call the API, show what it returns, and reveal the chosen preset (or visibly fall back to the synthetic benchmark). Right now the visitor can't tell whether the API was actually consulted.

openQuestions.title: Show the FPS-driven auto-downgrade pattern
openQuestions.detail: The page asserts 'Re-detecting per frame is wrong — the FPS counter is for measurement, not a feedback loop' — which is correct guidance. Pair it with an opt-in 'demonstrate runtime adaptation' toggle that calls the Compute Pressure API and lowers the preset on serious/critical pressure, so visitors learn both the rule and the legitimate exception.

openQuestions.title: Honour prefers-reduced-motion
openQuestions.detail: Particle bursts and shadow pulses run unconditionally. Add a `@media (prefers-reduced-motion)` branch that picks the low preset (or pauses entity motion) regardless of tier — this is the right interaction with platform accessibility settings.

summary: Strong canonical 'pick once, render hard' demo with a real canvas workload and an FPS readout — exactly the use case the spec was designed for. Add a visible auto-detect path and a reduced-motion branch to round it out.
